coredata.dc:title: High performance Fe and N-codoped graphene quantum dot supported Pd3Co catalyst with synergistically improved oxygen reduction activity and great methanol tolerance
coredata.dc:creator: Faraji, Monireh
Derakhshi, Pirouz
Tahvildari, Kambiz
Yousefian, Zohreh
coredata.dc:description: Designing high-performance and durable non-platinum catalysts as oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts is still a major barrier of fuel cell commercialization. In this work, simple hydrothermal and impregnation routes were applied to prepare non-platinum Pd-Co bimetallic nano-catalysts such as Fe-N doped graphene quantum dot (Fe-N-GQD) supported Pd3Co (Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD 10 wt%), carbon supported Pd3Co/C (10 wt%), graphene quantum dot supported Pd3Co/C (10 wt%). The synthesized catalysts were physico-chemically characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The electrochemical investigation was carried out in three electrode half-cell system to evaluate the catalyst activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the tolerance to methanol crossover and durability. In comparison to commercial Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%), the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD with lower weight percentage catalyst (∼10 wt%) displayed comparable electrocatalytic activity toward ORR with even higher methanol-tolerance capability and durability. The fabricated Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD with (10 wt %) metal loading exhibited only 20% lower activity than Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) toward ORR. Nevertheless the durability study of the catalyst in acidic media showed that the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD preserve 40% of its activity while Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) exhibited only 20% of its initial catalytic activity for ORR. Moreover the activity loss in the presence of methanol (0.1 M) was obtained for Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) and Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD 35% and 14%, respectively. To investigate the role of catalyst support, catalytic activities of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/GQD and Pd/Fe-N-GQD were compared. The results demonstrated superior catalytic activity of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD which could be related to the cocatalytic role of Fe-N-GQD due to the presence numerous of active sites exposed to the reactants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen reduction
Fe-N-Codoped graphene quantum dot
Pd-Co alloy
Methanol tolerance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synergetic effect for improved ORR electrocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) the UV–vis absorption spectra; b) the FT-IR spectra; c) Raman spectra of commercial few-layer graphene film and Fe-N-GQD; and d) XRD pattern of pristine graphene and Fe-N-GQD.
a) The survey XPS spectrums of Fe-N-GQD; b) the peak deconvolution of C1s; c) the peak deconvolution of Fe2p and d) N1s core level of Fe-N-GQD.
a,b)TEM images of the-prepared Fe-N-GQD and as prepared Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD.c,d) HRTEM image of Fe-N-GQD and the corresponding line profile analyses of the lattice fringes.
a) XRD patterns of the Pd/C,Pd3Co/C and Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD,Pd3Co/GQD, b) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of Fe-N-GQD, c) Nyquist plots of Fe-N-GQD, GQD and carbon black.

a) CV (scan rate 50 mV s−1; solid line: O2 saturated; dash line: N2 saturated; 0.5 M HClO4 solution) b)Selected area for calculation of ESA b) Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD c) Pd3Co/C d) Pt/C (ETEK 20 wt%). e) Pd3Co/GQD f) Pd/Fe-N-GQD.

a) LSV (scan rate 10 mV s−1with 1600 rpm, O2 saturated 0.5 M HClO4 solution) polarization curves of the Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, Pt/C (ETEK 20 wt%), Pd3Co/GQD and Pd/Fe-N-GQD catalysts; b) Tafel plots of oxygen reduction reaction of prepared catalyst At low current density region potential range (0.95–0.75 V vs Ag/AgCl) c) Koutecky – Levich plotsfitted from the RDE data at −0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl; d) chronoamperometric responses of Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, Pt/C (ETEK 20 wt%),Pd3Co/GQD and Pd/Fe-N-GQD catalysts at −0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl in 0.5 M HClO4.

Linear sweep voltammetries of oxygen reduction in the presence of different MeOH concentrations a) Pt/C (ETEK 20 wt%), b) Pd3Co/C (c) Pd3Co/Fe-N-QD; d) Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD(scan rate 10 mV s−1; 1200 rpm rotation speed; 0.5 M HClO4; O2 saturated.
Electrochemical Surface Area, Mass activity, Tafel slope, Half-wave and onset potential of ORR.

head.ce:title: High performance Fe and N-codoped graphene quantum dot supported Pd3Co catalyst with synergistically improved oxygen reduction activity and great methanol tolerance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Faculty, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O.code, 1651153311, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), West Blvd, Azadi Sport Complex, P.O. Box 14665-137, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing high-performance and durable non-platinum catalysts as oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts is still a major barrier of fuel cell commercialization. In this work, simple hydrothermal and impregnation routes were applied to prepare non-platinum Pd-Co bimetallic nano-catalysts such as Fe-N doped graphene quantum dot (Fe-N-GQD) supported Pd3Co (Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD 10 wt%), carbon supported Pd3Co/C (10 wt%), graphene quantum dot supported Pd3Co/C (10 wt%). The synthesized catalysts were physico-chemically characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The electrochemical investigation was carried out in three electrode half-cell system to evaluate the catalyst activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the tolerance to methanol crossover and durability. In comparison to commercial Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%), the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD with lower weight percentage catalyst (∼10 wt%) displayed comparable electrocatalytic activity toward ORR with even higher methanol-tolerance capability and durability. The fabricated Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD with (10 wt %) metal loading exhibited only 20% lower activity than Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) toward ORR. Nevertheless the durability study of the catalyst in acidic media showed that the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD preserve 40% of its activity while Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) exhibited only 20% of its initial catalytic activity for ORR. Moreover the activity loss in the presence of methanol (0.1 M) was obtained for Pt/C (ETEK, 20 wt%) and Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD 35% and 14%, respectively. To investigate the role of catalyst support, catalytic activities of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/GQD and Pd/Fe-N-GQD were compared. The results demonstrated superior catalytic activity of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD which could be related to the cocatalytic role of Fe-N-GQD due to the presence numerous of active sites exposed to the reactants.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid global population growth, increasing energy demand, and aggravation of the environmental pollution persuaded scientists to find sustainable, renewable and green energy sources. Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have attracted remarkable attention of research interest because of high yield of energy conversion and low operation temperature. The major challenges associated with DMFCs commercialization are the high cost and low sources of Platinum (Pt) and Platinum alloy used as the electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) occurring in cathode and anode side, respectively [1–5]. Another challenge of using Platinum catalyst in DMFC cathode is mixed potential generation due to the penetration of methanol from anode to cathode, commonly known as methanol crossover, which reduces the cell efficiency [6–12]. Among various strategies employed to replace Pt from cathode, Palladium (Pd) has been extensively studied due to being much less expensive and more abundant sources than Pt [13–18]. However, the catalytic activity of Pd toward ORR is 5-fold less than Pt, therefore the electronic structure of Pd catalyst should be modified to obtain high catalytic activity for ORR as much as Pt [19–24]. Alloying with other transition metal atoms like Ni, Co, Fe and Cu induces changes in electronic structure of Pd and makes it more efficient catalyst for ORR [15,25–32]. In particular, Pd-Co alloys catalysts present high current density, positive onset potential and excellent resistance to methanol crossover, and therefore could be promising alternatives to Pt for ORR in cathode catalyst layer of DMFCs [7,22,24,32–35].

In general, one of the drawbacks of Pd-based catalysts supported on carbon black, is the weak attachment of Pd nanoparticles to carbon based support materials, consequently the catalyst nanoparticles may simply dissolved, aggregate, and even leach during the cell working, resulting in performance lost [36,37]. To overcome the drawbacks of carbon black catalyst support, it should be replaced with new catalyst support with durable loading sites for electrocatalyst particles, electron and mass transfer pathways. An ideal catalyst support offers resistance to corrosion possesses strong interaction with catalyst particle which enhances ORR kinetics [38]. Recently, majority of the research focused on using doped graphene quantum dots (GQD) as catalyst material for ORR due to interesting properties such as rich electrocatalytic edging, high electrical conductivity, high surface area, and plenty of surface functional groups [39–43]. However up to now, a few research studied the GQDs as catalyst support for metal nanoparticles [40,41,43]. To the best of our knowledge, there is no conclusive information available on the effect of codoped GQD on catalytic activity of Pd-Co catalyst toward ORR and tolerance to MOR making it a topic worthy of investigation.

Hence, based on the previously published results [42–44] the idea of using GQDs as support material for Pd catalyst could be intersting in view of the fact that GQDs structural defects can help to build stronger electronic interaction between the metal nanoparticles and GQDs resulting the enhanced catalytic activity and stability [43].

Here in this work for the first time, we designed and synthesized Fe-N codoped GQDs through facile hydrothermal method and studied the influences of GQDs as catalyst support for Pd-Co nanoparticles for ORR. The Pd-Co alloy catalysts were prepared with the Pd:Co atomic ratio of 3:1 according to the best performance of PdxCoy/C electrocatalysts for ORR reported in Refs. [18,35,45].

The Fe-N-GQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method starting from citric acid (Merck) as the carbon precursor, urea (Merck) as the N source and ferric chloride FeCl3 (Merck) as the iron source. Citric acid (0.25 g), urea (0.16 g) and FeCl3 (0.2 g) were dissolved into 5 ml ultrapure deionized boiling water and kept stirring for 1 h, afterwards the obtained solution was transferred into a 100 ml autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. The prepared mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to discard the solid residue and attain the aqueous solution for further use. The aqueous solution was dialyzed with dialysis membrane overnight. The final solution was collected as the Fe-N-GQDs [44,46].

Pd3Co supported on Fe-N-GQDs and carbon black (Vulcan xc 72) were synthesized by impregnation method [36]. The preparation process of electrocatalysts was as following: Firstly the support materials (Fe-N-GQDs and carbon Vulcan xc 72) were added to the mixture of deionized water and isopropyl alcohol under vigorous ultrasonic probe assisted mixing for 40 min. Then PdCl2 (Merck) and CoCl2 (Merck), in atomic ratio of 3:1 (Pd/Co), were added to prepared suspensions under vigorous stirring. Ethylene glycol added as a reducing agent for achieving a total metal loading of 10 wt%. The pH value of mixture was adjusted to 10–11 with 1 M NaOH solution. The obtained mixture was refluxed overnight at 120 °C, then filtered several time centrifuged, washed with acetone and extra pure water and finally kept in a vacuum oven at 100 °C to gain the supported alloy electrocatalyst [36]. So as to study the role of N and Fe doping in the electrocatalytic activity of the hybrid, Pd3Co catalyst loaded on GQD and Vulacan Xr-72 were prepared and their electrocatalytic performance toward ORR investigated and compared with the Fe-N-GQD sample. The preparation methods for Pd3Co/GQD, Pd3Co/C and Pd/Fe-NGQD are extensively described in supplementary material.

Three working electrodes were fabricated from the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQDs, Pd3Co/C and commercial standard Pt/C catalysts (20 wt% ETEK), using Nafion solution as a binder in all electrodes. The desired amount of electrocatalyst and Nafion solution were dispersed in a 1:1 (v/v) of water/isopropanol solution and homogenized by sonication for 20 min at ambient conditions [47,48]. The loading of electrocatalyst and Nafion in catalyst layer were 10%wt and 20% wt. respectively. A glassy carbon electrode modified with the obtained catalyst suspension. For ORR investigations, the fabricated electrodes were soaked in an aqueous solution of 0.5 M HClO4, used as the electrolyte. N2 or O2 was used to purge the solution to achieve the O2-free or O2-saturated electrolyte solution. The kinetics study of ORR was done by rotating disk electrode (RDE) technique with a rotating speed of 600–1800 rpm. Electrochemical procedures such as linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and chronoamperometry (CA) investigations were carried out by potentiostat-galvanostat (Auto lab) in a standard three-electrode cell under ambient condition.

To investigate the morphology of prepared samples, high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (TEM) studies were performed with a Philips transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a diffractometer with a copper Kα radiation. Raman spectra recorded by a 514 nm laser. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of Fe-N-GQDs was recorded on PerkinElmer UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) evaluations were done with a PerkinElmer. FTIR spectrometer (Spectrum One) within a range of 800–4000 cm−1 with precision of ±1 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a radiation source of Al Kα (1486.6 eV) in an ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) system was used to study surface chemical compositions of the prepared support and catalyst materials. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using a Netzsch STA 449 F3 system.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was used to explore the optical properties of the Fe-N-GQDs. Fig. 1 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe-N-GQDs. It exhibits two absorption peaks, one peak at 238 nm which corresponds to
π →
π ∗
transition of aromatic sp2 region (C=C) and the other one a shoulder band at 347 nm associates with
n →
π ∗
transition of C=O [46]. Thus, the observed peaks in this absorption spectrum proves the successful GQD fabrication. Further investigation of Fe-N-GQDs functional groups were carried out by FTIR technique and results are given in Fig. 1b. The absorption peaks at 781 and 889 cm−1 are consistent with the vibrations of −CH2 plane. Additionally, the vibration peaks at 1174 and 1395 cm−1 can be related to COH/COC and C-N/C=N groups, respectively [44]. Raman spectra of the commercial few-layer graphene film (Graphene supermarket) and Fe-N-GQDs are presented in Fig. 1c for comparison. The observed peak for Fe-N-GQDs at 1359 and 1589 cm−1 are relevant to the D and G bands, respectively, of graphitic materials. It is observed that the graphene has ID/IG ratio of 0.8 lower than that of the Fe-N-GQDs (∼1.06) which is an evidence for fewer defect in graphene structure. Successful N-doping occurrence was subsequently confirmed by the downshift of the G peak from 1598 (cm−1) in graphene to 1589 (cm−1) in Fe-N-GQDs. Furthermore, broader D band of Fe-N-GQD proposes that the intercalation of N atoms into the conjugated carbon skeletal results in slightly disordered structures [49,50]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD pattern of the few layer graphene film and Fe-N-GQDs show typical (002) peaks of carbons materials at 24.4ᶱ and 25.5ᶱ, respectively. The weak broad (002) peak of Fe-N-GQD is ascribed to the thinness and disordered stacking of some Fe-N-GQD. The d spacing for Fe-N-GQD (0.38 nm) is larger than d spacing for graphene (0.34 nm) suggesting the more compact structure and
π − π
stacking of few-layer graphene film than Fe-N-GQD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigations were conducted to obtain surface information, elemental composition and Fe and N bonding configuration of the as prepared Fe-N-GQD. The attained results are presented in Fig. 2 a–d. The survey spectra of Fe-N-GQD (Fig. 2a) shows that the peaks located at binding energies of 284.8, 400.0, 533.3 and 720.5 eV are attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2P3, respectively. High resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) illustrates five different kind of peaks centered at 283.6, 284.5, 285.9, 288.2 and 290.2 eV corresponding to sp2, sp3 hybridized carbon, C=N, C-O, and C=O, respectively. As seen in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution Fe2p XPS spectrum displays five peaks at 710.5, 714.1, 719.4, 723.2, and 724.9 eV. The peak at 719.4 eV is satellite peak for other four peaks. The peak at 714.1 and 709.9 eV can be allocated to the 2p 3/2 of Fe(II) ion and Fe(III) ion, respectively, implying the presence of both Fe(II) ion and Fe(III) ion in prepared Fe-N-GQD. The peak at 725.2 eV is assigned to the binding energies of 2p1/2 of Fe(III) and Fe(II) ion, the peaks at 723.2 eV can be attributed to the binding energies of 2p 1/2 of Fe(II) ion. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be deconvoluted to three types of peaks: pyridinic N (399.1), pyrrolic N (400.3) and graphitic N (401.5) [51]. Incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon structure can remarkably improve the electrical conductivity of carbon materials leading to better ORR performance [52,53]. It is worth noting that N doping to carbon structures results in enhanced ORR activity, the percentage of this enhancement strongly depends on the role and amount of various kind of N heteroatom. For example, Ruff et al. [54] reported that graphitic N improves limiting current density whereas pyridinic-N reduces the ORR over-potential and catalyzes the reaction along the 4-electron pathway. In addition, the calculated amount of each element in Fe-N-GQD is C (60.5%), O (24.3%), N (12.3%) and Fe (2.9%), respectively.

HRTEM image (Fig. 3a) shows that the prepared Fe-N-GQDs has nearly spherical morphology. TEM images of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQDs (Fig. 3b) evidence the formation of the fine particles of Pd3Co with the average value of 3.5 ± 0.5 nm and uniform dispersion. Moreover, most of the Pd3Co particles adhere to the surface of Fe-N-GQD separately and no drastic aggregation can be observed in the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQDs samples. The resultant HRTEM image of Fe-N-GQDs (Fig. 3d) exhibits a good crystalline structure and the in-plane lattice spacing of 0.355 nm is corresponded to the (002) planes of graphite.

The elemental compositions of the prepared Pd-Co electrocatalysts were examined by inductively coupled plasma)ICP(analysis. The ICP result (Table S1) showed that the prepared electrocatalyst (atomic ratio) was remarkably close to the target stoichiometric values (Pd3:Co:1). The TGA results in Fig. S1 show that the total weight loss of prepared catalyst, which is corresponding to their carbon content, is about 90% at temperatures of up to 500 °C. The bare carbon black is decayed entirely at 1000 °C, signifying high purity of the carbon black. The loading of metal in all prepared catalyst is about 10% as seen in Table1S.

The crystalline structure of the fabricated catalysts were obtained by means of powder XRD. Fig. 4 a displays the X-ray diffraction patterns of the carbon (Vulcan xc 72) supported, GQD and “Fe-N-GQD”supported catalysts. Based on the ASTM 05–0681 reference, all the prepared catalysts mainly show the features of a single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) crystalline phase. Owing to the relatively strong signal for the Pd particles, no evidence of diffraction peaks of cobalt was identified in the Pd3Co catalysts. The first peak at
2 θ =
28.5
°
in both XRD patterns attributed to carbon support for the electrocatalyst. The observed diffraction 40.13°, 46.45°, 68.09°, and 81.21° of
2 θ
, which are respectively ascribed to (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystal face of Pd metal. As seen in XRD patterns the three main diffraction peaks for Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/GQD and Pd3Co/Fe-N-QD alloy electrocatalyst are somewhat shifted to higher
2 θ
amount which caused by lattice contraction of Pd by incorporation of Co. This angle shift confirms the formation of alloy electrocatalyst. The crystalline size of Pd3Co was estimated based on the Debye Scherrer equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
L =
0.9 λ
β
1 2
cos
θ
by fitting the Pd (220) peak.
λ
is the wavelength of the X-ray (1.54060), θ is the incident angle at the peak maximum and
β
1 2
is the full width at half maximum intensity of the peak (in Rad). The obtained crystallin size for Pd/C, Pd/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/GQD and Pd3Co/Fe-N-QD are 4, 3.5, 3.6, 3.5 and 3.2, respectively.

In order to further identify the porous feature and specific surface area of Fe-N-GQDs, N2 adsorption–desorption measurement was also carried out at 78 K. Fig. 4b shows the typical IV isotherms of Fe-N-GQDs and the hysteresis loop in the curve specifies the presence of a mesoporous structure. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of the Fe-N-GQDs is measured to be 266 m2g−1with a pore volume of 0.40 cm3g-1 implying that Fe-N-GQDs has large surface area. The predominant pore size and average pore size calculated on the basis of the Barrett–Joyer–Halenda (BJH) model obtained 5 and 14 nm, respectively, which can demonstrate the highly dispersion of Fe-N-GQDs pores.

Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to compare electrical conductivity of prepared support materials (Fe-N-GQD and GQD) with carbon black (Vulcan xc72). The equivalent circuit model used to interpret the obtained data and the corresponding Nyquist plots were presented in Fig. 4c. The Nyquist plots show a semicircles in high frequency region and straight lines in low frequency region. The high frequency region is responsible for charge transfer resistance hence the charge transfer resistance can be obtained from semicircle diameter. As seen in Fig. 4c the semicircle related to the Fe-N-GQD modified electrode had a lower diameter than semicircles for the GQD and Vulcan xc 72 modified electrodes, indicating that charge transfer resistance (Rct) value of Fe-N-GQD (135 Ω) than that of Vulcan xc72 (261 Ω) and GQD (420 Ω) exhibited higher electrical conductivity for the electron transfer between Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. These results suggest that Fe, N doping dramatically increases graphene quantum dot electrical conductivity and makes it a suitable candidate as a catalyst support.

The electrocatalytic activity of fabricated electrodes for ORR first examined with Cyclic voltammetry (CV)·CV curves of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/C, Pd/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/GQD and commercial Pt/C (20 wt %) obtained under N2 saturated in 0.5 M HClO4 electrolyte from −0.2 to 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl with the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 are presented in Fig. 5 a. CV was performed to compare the electrochemical activities for the prepared catalysts. The obtained voltammograms were recorded after 50 cycles, since first 50 cycles were conducted to activate the surface of electrodes. As shown in Fig. 5a, CV behavior of all catalyst are similar. The hydrogen adsorption-desorption peaks, Pt and Pd reduction-oxidation peaks are remarkably observed, indicating the presence of Pt and Pd. The calculation of the electrochemical surface area (ESA) of Pd catalyst is not as facile as in the case of Pt, which can be obtained with the determination of the charge density related with the adsorption of one monolayer of H UPD [55].

For Pd electrodes the ESA was calculated from the charge of the monolayer of oxygenated species, which was assessed from the area of the Pd oxide reduction peak [56]. The charge of the oxygen monolayer (QO) for a Pd electrode is twofold larger than the hydrogen monolayer (QH) in Pt, QH = 1/2 QO (QH = 210 μCcm−2) (Fig. 5b–f). The calculated ESA values for all electrocatalysts are presented in Table 1 .

The voltammetric features of the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD is similar to that obtained for Pd3Co/C, however, certain significant variances can be observed between Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD and Pd3Co/C. The peak at 0.051 V on Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD is more expanded to higher potential and has the increased double layer charge relative to that Pd3Co/C. With Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD catalyst, the current attributable to formation of the oxide species initiates at lower potential and it has additionally increased from Pd3Co/C catalysts in the potential region of 0.4–0.65 V.

In addition cyclic voltammetric behavior of support materials, Fe-N-GQD and GQD, were investigated under N2 and O2 atmosphere. In N2 environment, there is no clear faradic current peak in CV curves of both Fe-N-GQD and GQD support material (dashed lines in Fig. 2S). On the other hand an evident cathodic current peak appears related to ORR in Fe-N-GQD CV curve under O2 saturation (solid lines in Fig. 2S) whereas no ORR activity is observed in GQD CV curve. These findings indicate that Fe-N-GQD in addition to a support role has catalytic activity toward ORR. This catalytic activity of support material is reported by Xioa et al. [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic effect for improved ORR electrocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: Studding for rotating disk electrode (RDE) modified with the prepared catalyst were then performed by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in O2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4. Fig. 6 and Table 1 present the ORR performance of prepared electrodes. As shown in Fig. 5a, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) plot of the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD catalyst demonstrates improved onset potential (Eonset), half-wave potential (E1/2) and limiting current density in comparison with other prepared catalysts.

Although the commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) presents a higher activity than the Pd-based catalysts, the Eonset (−0.92 V) and E1/2 (−0.7 V) of the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD catalyst are only 20 mV negatively shifted, respectively, compared to the Pt/C (ETEK 20 wt%) catalyst (Eonset = −0.94 V, E1/2 = −0.78 V). Moreover, the obtained limiting current density for Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD reaches 6.7 mAcm−2 which is close to the commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) (6.8 mAcm−2). The obtained results propose the improved ORR electrocatalytic activity of the Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD sample in acidic electrolyte. Fig. 6b exhibits the mass-corrected Tafel plots of the ORR voltammograms on prepared electrodes. The Tafel slope of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD (68 mV per decade) over the high potential range is very close to the values of the Pd/C (70 mV per decade) and Pt/C (65 mV per decade) catalysts, indicating nearly the same ORR mechanism of the different catalysts. The mass activity (MA) which is the current per unit mass of the catalyst is calculated from the background-corrected ORR voltammograms is reported in Table 1. The MA is calculated by normalizing the mass-transport-corrected kinetic current with catalyst loading and active area of the catalyst on the electrode. The calculated MA for Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD is higher than that of Pd3Co/C. but almost analogous with that of Pt/C. The better performance of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD in comparison with Pd3Co/C and Pd3Co/GQD reveals the essential impact of nitrogen and iron incorporation on enhancing the catalytic activity of the hybrid material. Rapid charge transfer and efficient catalytic active sites can be obtained owing to the high electrical conductivity of Fe-N-GQD as investigated in EIS studies Fig 2S.

In order to understand the effect of metal catalyst composition on catalytic activity for ORR, The electrochemical parameters obtained for Pd/Fe-NGQD (Table 1 and Fig. 6) was compared to Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD. The obtained Eonset, E1/2, ESA for Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD is higher than Pd/Fe-NGQD whereas the calculated Tafel slop for Pd/Fe-NGQD is lower than for Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD indicating faster kinetics of ORR on Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD.

In addition, the ORR mechanism occurred on prepared catalysts was investigated with RDE. The electron transfer numbers of the prepared catalysts can be calculated by the Koutecky-Levich equation (Eq. (2)):
(2)
1 J
=
1
J
k i n
+
1
J
d i f f
=
1
J
k i n
+
1
B
ω
where
B = 0.62 n F
D
2 3
ϑ
− 1
6
C 0
and
J
k i n
= n F k
C 0

The current density
(
J
( m A c
m
− 2
)
)
includes a kinetic part
(
J
k i n
( m A c
m
− 2
)
)
and a diffusion part
(
J
d i f f
( m A c
m
− 2
)
)
, ω is the angular velocity of the rotating electrode
(
ω = 2 π N
)
where N is the linear rotation speed. B is a constant, its relation with electron transfer number n. Where n is the number of electrons transferred during the electrochemical reaction, F is the Faraday constant (F = 96485 Cmol-1), D is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in the 0.5 M HClO4 electrolyte (D = 1.93 × 10−5 cm2s−1), C
0 is the bulk concentration of O2 (C = 1.26 × 10−3 mol L−1), ϑ is the kinematic viscosity of the electrolyte ( ϑ =1.009 × 10−2cm2s−1) [58]. Plotting
J
− 1
versus
ω
− 1
2
illustrates a line at each potential.

There are two possible multi electron transfer pathway for ORR: one is four-electrons transfer one step direct pathway, leading to generate H2O directly; the other one is two steps two-electrons transfer indirect route, leading to produce H2O2 primarily and then the H2O2 converts to H2O by receiving another two electrons. Mostly, the efficiency of the two-electron pathway is less than the four electron path way, consequently, the favorable catalyst for the ORR is the catalyst on which the ORR occurrs in direct four electron route.

According to the Koutecky-Levich curves (Fig. 6c), the electron transfer numbers of the, Pd3Co/GQD, Fe-N-GQD, Pd/Fe-N-GQD, Pd3Co/C, Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD, and commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) catalysts were calculated 2.9, 3.1, 3.2, 3.28, 3.5, 4.02, 4.1, respectively, denoting that the ORR performs on Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD and commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) catalysts toward the four-electrons transfer route. The electron transfer number for Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD is higher than other catalysts implies that Fe-N-GQD participate in ORR process.

The durability of the above mentioned catalysts for ORR catalytic activity was studied by means of continuous chronoamperometric measurements under O2 saturated atmosphere at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl in a 0.5 M HClO4 electrolyte (Fig. 6d). After the 6000 s reaction time, the Pt/C catalysts demonstrates percentage of performance loss with 40% of the relative current persevered in acidic electrolyte; whereas, the current density of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD and Pd3Co/C catalyst have minor loss, and the activity deficiency were only 10.6% and 20 wt% in acidic medium. We propose that the high durability of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD may be originated from the stable structure of Fe-N-GQD than GQD and carbon black.

One of the major sources of activity loss in ORR catalysts and cell voltage drop in DMFC fuel cells is the crossover of methanol (MeOH) from the anode to the cathode. An ideal electrocatalyst for cathode in DMFC should have sufficient oxygen reduction activity and low methanol oxidation activity. Since Pd-based electrocatalysts have been reported in previous works [59–61] as electrocatalysts which meet these requirements, in this work two approach, alloying with Co and anchoring to Fe-N-GQD support material, are employed to modify the catalyst activity to ORR and tolerance to methanol oxidation and durability in fuel cell operation condition. Fig. 7 shows the Linear sweep voltammetries of prepared catalysts catalysts coated on glassy carbon electrodes in 0.5 M HClO4 in the potential range between 0.2 and 0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrolytes at various methanol concentrations under O2 atmosphere. It is well known that Pt is a great electrocatalyst for the ORR and similarly for methanol oxidation; as a result it has been employed as a standard catalyst for comparison. Fig. 7a shows a methanol oxidation peak in 0.65 V and the onset potential around 0.5 V for Pt/C electrode. Higher peak currents emerge for higher methanol concentrations. It should be noted that Pt has catalytic activity for ORR in the same potential range for MOR. Therefore, ORR and MOR are competitive reactions on Pt/C electrode. This phenomena can be plausibly explained by the adsorption of methanol or some other toxic intermediates generated during the methanol oxidation. In contrast, no distinct methanol oxidation peak is observed on the other catalysts (Fig. 7b and c) for all methanol concentrations. Based on these voltamograms it is evident that Pd3Co/Fe-N-QD and Pd3Co/C have no catalytic activity toward methanol oxidation.

In summary, the Pd3Co alloy nanoparticle supported on Fe-and N-doped graphene quantum dot (Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD) were successfully fabricated through the facile hydrothermal method. The Pd3Co alloy catalyst, with 3–4 nm narrow size distribution, demonstrated an ultrahigh uniform distribution on Fe-N-GQD. It was found that the prepared Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD exhibited similar half wave potential and-onset potential similar to commercial Pt/C (20 wt%). The superior electrocatalytic activity of Pd3Co/Fe-N-GQD toward the ORR might be ascribed to the synergistic effect from the distinctive structures and chemical compositions: (I) the homogeneous dispersion of ultrafine Pd3Co nanoparticles in Fe-N-GQD with a high specific surface area supplies copious active and fully reachable catalytic sites. (II) The composite structure of bimetallic Pd3Co nanoprticles on Fe-N-GQD supports strong interface interaction between the Pd3Co and catalyst support during potential cycling leads to better charge transfer. (c) The porous structure of Fe-N-GQD facilitates the accesiblity of reactants to catalyst (III) Incorporation of Co to Pd crystal structure can modify the electronic structure of Pd, which could efficiently improve the electrochemical activity. The excellent durability and methanol tolerance of Pd-Co alloy catalysts present them as an alternative catalyst materials to commercial Pt/C, hence a promising perspective in DMFC fuel cells.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pt-decorated Pd/C catalysts for methanol-tolerance oxygen reduction reaction

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: C. Lo Vecchio, D. Sebastián, C. Alegre, A.S. Aricò, V. Baglio, J. Electroanal. Chem.
